source.title: Lactate biosensors for spectrally and spatially multiplexed fluorescence imaging

initial_request.research_problem: Develop improved green extracellular and red intracellular L-lactate biosensors with higher ΔF/F, faster kinetics, and better localization for multiplexed imaging.
initial_request.known_context: eLACCO1.1 suffers from aggregation and limited fluorescence response on mammalian cell surface; no red fluorescent biosensors for intracellular L-lactate exist.
constraints: Must enable spectrally and spatially multiplexed imaging
quantifiable_goals: Red intracellular sensor with high ΔF/F

evolution_trajectory.state_before: eLACCO1.1 aggregates and has limited fluorescence response on mammalian cell surface; no red intracellular L-lactate biosensor exists.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need improved green extracellular sensor and a new red intracellular sensor for multiplexed imaging.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A spectrally and functionally orthogonal pair of green extracellular and red intracellular biosensors can be developed.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design orthogonal pair: green extracellular eLACCO2.1 and red intracellular R-iLACCO1. Select eLACCO0.9 as starting point for green sensor evolution due to faster kinetics than eLACCO1.
evolution_trajectory.observation: eLACCO1.1 aggregation and limited response; no red intracellular sensor reported. eLACCO0.9 identified as promising candidate.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to directed evolution of eLACCO0.9.

evidence.quote_or_span: the first-in-class eLACCO1.1 suffers from aggregation and limited fluorescence response on the surface of mammalian cells
no red fluorescent biosensors for intracellular L-lactate have yet been reported, hindering imaging with other green fluorescent biosensors in spatially and spectrally multiplexed manner

a spectrally and functionally orthogonal pair of high-performance genetically encoded biosensors: the second-generation green fluorescent eLACCO2.1 for extracellular L-lactate and the first-generation red fluorescent R-iLACCO1 for intracellular L-lactate
we first identified eLACCO0.9 as a promising candidate due to a faster fluorescence response than eLACCO1

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to improve ΔF/F and other properties.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Directed evolution can yield variants with higher ΔF/F.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform directed evolution: 8 rounds of random mutagenesis on eLACCO0.9 to produce eLACCO2 with ΔF/F 16.

evidence.quote_or_span: Leu79Ile mutation was added to eLACCO2 to tune the L-lactate affinity. The resulting mutant was designated as eLACCO2.1.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to tune affinity for physiological lactate range.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Introduce Leu79Ile into eLACCO2 to produce eLACCO2.1 (Kd 1.9 mM, ΔF/F 14).
evolution_trajectory.observation: eLACCO2.1 has Kd 1.9 mM and ΔF/F 14.

evidence.quote_or_span: Leu79Ile mutation was added to eLACCO2 to tune the L-lactate affinity. The resulting mutant was designated as eLACCO2.1.
Finally, Leu79Ile mutation was added to eLACCO2 to tune the L-lactate affinity.
Of ten variants investigated, eLACCO2 Leu79Ile, designated eLACCO2.
1 has a $\Delta F/F$ of 14 as a purified protein upon treatment with 10 mM L-lactate, which is 3.5-fold higher than eLACCO1.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: eLACCO2.1 requires optimal leader/anchor for surface localization.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to identify best leader and anchor for eLACCO2.1 surface localization and ΔF/F.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening leaders and anchors will identify optimal combination.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen 23 leaders and 10 anchors; select HA leader and NGR anchor for eLACCO2.1.
evolution_trajectory.observation: HA leader + NGR anchor provided very good membrane localization and greater fluorescent signal.

evidence.quote_or_span: influenza hemagglutinin (HA) provided very good membrane localization and also showed greater fluorescent signal relative to CD59-eLACCO1.1-NGR in neurites
the first-in-class eLACCO1.1 suffers from aggregation and limited fluorescence response on the surface of mammalian cells

evolution_trajectory.state_before: eLACCO2.1 with HA leader and NGR anchor.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to validate performance in mammalian cells.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: eLACCO2.1 will show improved ΔF/F and kinetics in HeLa cells.
evolution_trajectory.observation: eLACCO2.1 shows improved performance in HeLa cells.

evidence.quote_or_span: 1 has a $\Delta F/F$ of 14 as a purified protein upon treatment with 10 mM L-lactate, which is 3.5-fold higher than eLACCO1.
the first-in-class eLACCO1.1 suffers from aggregation and limited fluorescence response on the surface of mammalian cells

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does eLACCO2.1 detect endogenous lactate changes in brain tissue?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: eLACCO2.1 will respond to aglycemia and theta burst stimulation in brain slices.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test eLACCO2.1 in acute brain slices.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Fluorescence decreased upon aglycemia and increased upon theta burst stimulation.

evidence.quote_or_span: For aglycemia experiments in acute brain slices, glucose was not added to the ACSF
The tissue was stimulated with 1 ms long mono-phasic pulses at 1.2 V using a theta burst pattern
For aglycemia experiments in acute brain slices, glucose was not added to the ACSF
The tissue was stimulated with 1 ms long mono-phasic pulses at 1.2 V using a theta burst pattern

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Low ΔF/F; need to improve response.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Delete 2 aa in C-terminal linker → R-iLACCO0.2 (ΔF/F 0.6).

evidence.quote_or_span: N- and C-terminal linkers of R-iLACCO0.1 were deleted using Q5 high-fidelity DNA polymerase (New England Biolabs) to provide variants with different linker length. Variants were expressed in E. coli. Proteins were extracted using B-PER bacterial protein extraction reagent (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and tested for fluorescence brightness and L-lactate-dependent response. The most promising variant, designated as R-iLACCO0.2

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need much higher ΔF/F for practical use.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Directed evolution can significantly improve ΔF/F.
evolution_trajectory.decision: 11 rounds of directed evolution (1 linker + 10 random) → R-iLACCO1 (ΔF/F 20).
evolution_trajectory.observation: R-iLACCO1 achieved ΔF/F 20, high molecular brightness 11.3.

evidence.quote_or_span: we performed one round of C-terminal linker optimization, followed by ten rounds of directed evolution by random mutagenesis of the whole gene

R-iLACCO0.2, was subjected to an iterative process of library generation and screening in E. coli. Libraries were generated by site-directed random mutagenesis or error-prone PCR of the whole gene. For each round, approximately 200–400 fluorescent colonies were picked, cultured, and tested on 96-well plates under a plate reader. There were 11 rounds of screening before R-iLACCO1, R-iLACCO1.1, and R-iLACCO1.2 were identified.

Ultimately, we developed the R-iLACCO1 variant with high molecular brightness (11.3 in L-lactate-bound state, Table 2) and $\Delta F/F$ of 20 that is one of the highest fluorescence responses for red fluorescent biosensor protein, as measured using purified protein (Supplementary Table 2) $^{18,31}$ .

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need variants with lower affinity for physiological lactate range.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening at multiple lactate concentrations will identify lower affinity variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen final round at 0, 0.5, and 10 mM lactate → R-iLACCO1.1 and 1.2.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Identified R-iLACCO1.1 and R-iLACCO1.2 with lower apparent Kd.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate best variant in mammalian cells.

evidence.quote_or_span: R-iLACCO0.2, was subjected to an iterative process of library generation and screening in E. coli. Libraries were generated by site-directed random mutagenesis or error-prone PCR of the whole gene. For each round, approximately 200–400 fluorescent colonies were picked, cultured, and tested on 96-well plates under a plate reader. There were 11 rounds of screening before R-iLACCO1, R-iLACCO1.1, and R-iLACCO1.2 were identified.
In the final round of directed evolution, we screened all randomly picked variants in the absence of L-lactate, at 0.5 mM L-lactate, and at 10 mM L-lactate, to assess their apparent $K_{d}$ values.
The tissue was stimulated with 1 ms long mono-phasic pulses at 1.2 V using a theta burst pattern
The tissue was stimulated with 1 ms long mono-phasic pulses at 1.2 V using a theta burst pattern

evolution_trajectory.state_before: R-iLACCO1.2 identified as lower affinity variant.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to confirm performance in mammalian cells.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: R-iLACCO1.2 will show best performance in HeLa and other cells.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Characterize R-iLACCO1.2 in HeLa, HEK293T, neurons, astrocytes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: R-iLACCO1.2 shows best performance, uniform localization, no punctate accumulation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate in primary neurons and astrocytes with MCT inhibition.

evidence.quote_or_span: Bold represents the best performance of the lactate biosensors investigated.
We also confirmed that R-iLACCO1 and its affinity variants display no undesirable punctate intracellular accumulation

HeLa (American Type Culture Collection, ATCC, #CCL-2) and HEK293T (Thermo Fisher Scientific, #R70007) cells were maintained in Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM; Nakalai Tesque) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS; Sigma-Aldrich) and 1% penicillin–streptomycin (Nakalai Tesque) at 37 °C and 5% CO $_{2}$ .
# Imaging of eLACCO and R-iLACCO variants in HeLa, HEK293T, and T98G cell lines
2 showed the largest fluorescence response of all R-iLACCO variants and its $\Delta F/F$ (2.5 ± 0.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: R-iLACCO variants characterized in cell lines.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do they function in primary neurons and astrocytes?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: R-iLACCO variants will respond to MCT inhibition in primary cells.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test R-iLACCO variants in primary neurons and astrocytes with AR-C155858.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Robust increase in fluorescence intensity upon MCT inhibition.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to in vivo validation of eLACCO2.1.

evidence.quote_or_span: Treatment of neurons and astrocytes with AR-C155858 yielded a robust increase in the fluorescence intensity of R-iLACCO variants

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: eLACCO2.1 will respond to i.c.v. lactate injection in awake mice.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform in vivo fiber photometry with i.c.v. lactate injection.
evolution_trajectory.observation: eLACCO2.1 fluorescence increased upon lactate injection; deLACCO1 control did not respond.

evidence.quote_or_span: eLACCO fluorescence was acquired by the fiber photometry system
eLACCO fluorescence was acquired by the fiber photometry system

validation_methods: In vitro characterization of purified proteins
Live-cell imaging in HeLa, HEK293T, T98G, primary neurons, astrocytes
In vivo fiber photometry in mice
success_verification.final_conclusion: Developed eLACCO2.1 (green extracellular) and R-iLACCO1 series (red intracellular) with improved ΔF/F, kinetics, and localization, enabling multiplexed lactate imaging.
limitations: R-iLACCO variants exhibit slight blue-light sensitivity
Brightness and photostability could be further improved

risk_notes: Some evidence quotes are indirect (e.g., EV_AUTO_022 for HeLa ΔF/F).
Missing direct evidence for multiplexed imaging and organelle targeting steps, but these are not required for core trajectory.
